How We Organize Ourselves

Classroom Presidential Election Simulation

How do democratic systems organize voice, leadership, rights, and responsibilities?

Our class will create original parties, choose candidates, campaign respectfully, and vote responsibly.

Our Big Idea

Systems reflect values.

  • A government is a system for organizing people, decisions, rights, and responsibilities.
  • A democratic election gives people a voice in choosing leaders.
  • In this simulation, we will practice fairness, responsibility, respectful communication, and informed voting.

What We Are NOT Doing

This is a classroom simulation, not a real-world political debate.

  • We will not use real political parties.
  • We will not copy real politicians.
  • We will not insult people, pressure voters, or make unrealistic promises.
  • We will focus on classroom and school community issues.
Our goal is to understand systems, not to argue about real politics.

Our Class Election System

20 students, one organized process.

  • 4 official presidential candidates.
  • 4 original political parties.
  • 4 students in each party.
  • 4 neutral election officials.
  • Election Day will be Thursday of Week 4.

How We Will Choose Candidates

Students choose. The teacher does not choose.

  • Anyone interested may complete a Candidate Interest Form.
  • Interested students give a 30-second nomination speech.
  • The class votes privately in a primary election.
  • Each voter chooses 2 students they believe would be strong candidates.
  • The top 4 vote-getters become the official presidential candidates.

What Makes a Good Leader?

Leadership is service, not popularity.

  • A leader listens before deciding.
  • A leader treats people fairly.
  • A leader communicates respectfully.
  • A leader keeps promises realistic.
  • A leader serves the whole community, not only friends.
Think about the IB Learner Profile: principled, caring, open-minded, communicator, and reflective.
20 minutes

Week 3 Monday

Leadership Criteria and Candidate Interest

Today we decide what kind of leadership our classroom community needs.

Monday: Think First

Question for everyone

  • What should a president be responsible for in a classroom society?
  • What should a president not have power over?
  • How should a president treat people who disagree?

Monday: Candidate Interest Form

Only students who want to be considered complete this.

  • Why do I want to serve as class president?
  • What kind of classroom community do I want to help build?
  • What leadership qualities do I have?
  • How will I listen to people who disagree with me?
  • Which Learner Profile attribute will guide my leadership?

Monday: Exit Reflection

Complete this sentence.

Leadership is not about being popular. Leadership is about...
Be honest and thoughtful. This reflection helps us remember the purpose of the election.
20 minutes

Week 3 Tuesday

Nomination Speeches

Today every interested student gets a fair voice.

Tuesday: Campaign Norms

Before anyone speaks, we agree to these expectations.

  • We listen respectfully.
  • We do not laugh at or interrupt a speaker.
  • We do not insult people.
  • We do not make promises we cannot keep.
  • We vote based on leadership, values, and ideas, not popularity.

Tuesday: 30-Second Speech Structure

Candidates, use this structure.

  • My name is...
  • I want to serve because...
  • One value I care about is...
  • One way I would help our classroom community is...
Keep it short, clear, and respectful.

Tuesday: Voter Reflection

Before the primary vote, think carefully.

  • Which candidates showed responsibility?
  • Which candidates had clear ideas?
  • Which candidates communicated respectfully?
  • Which candidates seemed ready to listen to others?
20 minutes

Week 3 Wednesday

Primary Election

Today we narrow the field to 4 official presidential candidates.

Wednesday: What Is a Primary?

A primary is not the final election.

  • A primary helps narrow many possible candidates into a smaller group.
  • This makes the final election more organized.
  • Everyone still participates as voters, party members, or election officials.

Wednesday: Voting Rules

Private, fair, and thoughtful.

  • Each student votes for 2 students.
  • Vote for people you believe would be strong candidates.
  • Do not tell others who to vote for.
  • Do not pressure anyone.
  • The top 4 vote-getters become official presidential candidates.

Wednesday: If There Is a Tie

We use a fair tie-breaker.

  • If there is a tie for 4th place, only the tied students participate in a tie-breaker.
  • Each tied student gives one 20-second statement.
  • The class votes privately again between only those tied students.

Wednesday: Reflection

Answer after the primary.

How did this primary system help organize many voices into a smaller decision?
20 minutes

Week 3 Thursday

Original Political Parties and Leadership Teams

Today candidates begin building teams around shared values.

Thursday: What Is a Political Party?

A party is a group organized around shared values and goals.

  • Each party must be original.
  • Each party must represent classroom community values.
  • Each party must create its own name, symbol, slogan, and platform.
  • No party may copy or imitate an existing real-world political party.

Thursday: Party Leadership Roles

Each party will have 4 members.

  • President: main candidate and vision speaker.
  • Vice President: leadership partner and support speaker.
  • Campaign Manager: keeps the team organized.
  • Policy Advisor or Communications Director: helps make proposals realistic and clear.

Thursday: Choosing a Vice President

Choose for leadership fit, not friendship alone.

  • Who balances your strengths?
  • Who can respectfully challenge your thinking?
  • Who is responsible and reliable?
  • Who can help represent your values?
  • Who could step in and lead if needed?

Thursday: Role Interest Form

Everyone who is not a presidential candidate completes this.

  • Choose your top 3 preferred roles.
  • Explain what strengths you would bring.
  • Think about where you can help the system work best.
  • Some students will join parties and some will become neutral election officials.
20 minutes

Week 3 Friday

Party Identity and Platform

Today each party explains what it stands for and what it wants to improve.

Friday: Party Identity

Each party creates these pieces.

  • Original party name.
  • Party symbol.
  • Party slogan.
  • Three core values.
  • A short mission statement.

Friday: What Is a Platform?

A platform is a set of ideas a party wants to work on.

  • Your platform must include 3 realistic proposals.
  • Each proposal must help the classroom or school community.
  • Each proposal must connect to fairness, responsibility, voice, organization, kindness, service, learning, or community.

Friday: Proposal Planning Questions

For each proposal, answer these questions.

  • What problem are we addressing?
  • Why does it matter?
  • Who would benefit?
  • What is one realistic action we could take?
  • Which right or responsibility connects to this?

Friday: Promises We Cannot Make

Stay realistic and fair.

  • No promises about candy, money, or prizes.
  • No promises about grades.
  • No promises about unlimited free time.
  • No promises about removing classroom expectations.
  • No promises that target or exclude specific students.
20 minutes

Week 4 Monday

Campaign Materials

Today parties turn their ideas into respectful campaign messages.

Monday: Persuasion or Manipulation?

Know the difference.

  • Persuasion uses reasons, evidence, and clear ideas.
  • Manipulation uses pressure, exaggeration, or emotional tricks.
  • A responsible campaign persuades respectfully.

Monday: Campaign Materials Checklist

Each party creates these.

  • One campaign poster.
  • One short slogan.
  • One 45 to 60 second speech draft.
  • One campaign pledge.
  • One clear explanation of the 3 platform proposals.

Monday: Campaign Pledge

Every party must agree to this.

We promise to campaign respectfully, listen to others, and accept the results with maturity.
This pledge must be visible on your campaign materials.

Monday: Media and Fact Checker Job

Neutral officials protect the fairness of the system.

  • Check that promises are realistic.
  • Check that language is respectful.
  • Create neutral “Meet the Parties” summaries.
  • Do not support or criticize a party publicly.
20 minutes

Week 4 Tuesday

Campaign Speeches and Voter Education

Today voters listen for values, realistic ideas, and responsible leadership.

Tuesday: Speech Structure

President and vice president speak together.

  • Our party is...
  • Our values are...
  • Our three proposals are...
  • We believe this will help our class because...
  • We ask for your vote because...

Tuesday: Voter Checklist

Listen like an informed voter.

  • Does this party have clear ideas?
  • Are the ideas realistic?
  • Do the ideas serve the community?
  • Do the candidates communicate respectfully?
  • Do the candidates show the Learner Profile?

Tuesday: After Speeches

Complete your voter reflection.

  • One idea I liked was...
  • One question I still have is...
  • One value I heard was...
  • One responsibility voters have is...

Tuesday: Town Hall Questions

Submit questions about ideas, not personalities.

  • Ask about fairness.
  • Ask about realistic promises.
  • Ask about how they will listen.
  • Ask about how their plan helps the class.
  • Do not ask questions meant to embarrass someone.
20 minutes

Week 4 Wednesday

Town Hall

Today we practice respectful civic dialogue.

Wednesday: Civil Discourse Norms

How we speak matters.

  • Listen to understand.
  • Disagree respectfully.
  • Ask questions about ideas.
  • Use calm voices.
  • Give each party equal time.

Wednesday: Helpful Sentence Stems

Use these if you need them.

  • I respectfully disagree because...
  • Can you explain how...
  • What would you do if...
  • How would your plan help...
  • One concern I have is...

Wednesday: Town Hall Questions

Each party answers the same questions.

  • How will your party make sure every student feels heard?
  • What is one promise you know you can realistically keep?
  • How will you respond if classmates disagree with your ideas?
  • How does your platform show fairness and responsibility?

Wednesday: Final Statement

Each party gets 30 seconds.

  • Restate your values.
  • Name your strongest realistic proposal.
  • Thank the voters.
  • Remind everyone you will serve the whole class.

Wednesday: Quiet Reflection

No more campaigning after the town hall.

Tomorrow I will vote based on...
Use this time to think. Do not pressure others or ask who they will vote for.
20 minutes

Week 4 Thursday

Election Day

Today we vote privately, respectfully, and responsibly.

Election Day: Before Voting

Listen carefully to the procedure.

  • There is no campaigning today.
  • Do not ask anyone who they will vote for.
  • Do not tell anyone how to vote.
  • Your vote is private.
  • One student, one ballot, one vote.

Election Day: How to Vote

Follow each step calmly.

  • Wait until your name or group is called.
  • Take one ballot.
  • Go to the voting area privately.
  • Mark one president and vice president ticket.
  • Fold your ballot if using paper.
  • Place it in the ballot box.

Election Day: Ballot Rules

A ballot must be clear.

  • Choose only one ticket.
  • Do not write comments about other candidates.
  • Do not draw on the ballot.
  • If you make a mistake, quietly ask the Ballot Manager or teacher for help.
  • A ballot that is unclear may not be counted.

Election Day: Election Officials

Neutral officials help the system work fairly.

  • Election Commissioner reminds the class of rules.
  • Ballot Manager distributes and collects ballots.
  • Town Hall Moderator may help organize the line.
  • Media and Fact Checker observes fairness and prepares a neutral summary.

Election Day: Counting Votes

The count must be supervised and fair.

  • Election officials count with teacher supervision.
  • Votes are counted quietly.
  • The total is checked twice.
  • Results are announced respectfully.
  • Everyone accepts the result with maturity.

Election Day: After Results

The winner serves the whole class.

  • Thank all candidates and parties.
  • Thank all election officials.
  • Celebrate the process, not only the result.
  • Remember that leaders serve everyone, including people who voted differently.

Election Day: Final Reflection

Answer one or more prompts.

  • How did this election show that systems reflect values?
  • What responsibilities did candidates have?
  • What responsibilities did voters have?
  • How did our class protect fairness?
  • What did this teach us about democracy?

Closing Thought

Democracy depends on responsible participation.

A fair system works best when people use their voice with respect, listen to others, and take responsibility for the community.

Slide List
